How do we end my last tape here before I go live? You know I'm psyched to go live, I can do your Q&As and everything. So here's the deal. I picked a topic here that I really thought was interesting and that I thought would really be a great way to kind of sum up everything that we've accomplished and also why the straight line works so well, why you're getting the results that you're getting. I think I'm just really going to shed some light on that. So what I call this training here, I've come up with a name for it, it's called the overarching equations of the straight line. And I've mentioned this to you many times in passing, you know, I say this is overarching equations, I say like in the straight line, like what it does basically it takes all these things that are operating behind the scenes. Like all these things that for someone like myself that's like a natural born world class closer like all these things that are going on in my head and automatically get coagulated and spit out the right way. What the straight line basically does, it takes in all these rules into account and then allows you to essentially operate within that universe of rules. These are crucial things that essentially must happen to influence someone. So the straight line essentially operates within these boundaries of like all these rules and allows you to move forward from lack of certainty to absolute certainty without breaking any of the rules. Because if you break any of the rules, right, what happens? Well, then you can't close. So the first overarching rule, okay, and this is not so much about how the straight line operates. In other words, with the straight line system, and I use this metaphor in the past, this is a really good one, I think, right? Is that imagine that sales, right, at the highest level is the transference of emotion. That's what's happening. It's the transference of emotion. And the primary emotion that you're transferring is certainty, right? It's certainty about yourself, certainty about the product, and certainty about the company that stands behind the product, right? The three tens, pretty basic stuff, right? And you're transferring that certainty to your prospect, right? So essentially, how does that actually happen? Where does that certainty come from? Does that, all right, that's got to stop. That's got to stop, okay? All right, no more clicking, right? So I'm going to go cut that on you there, right? So where does that certainty come from? Okay, so where does that certainty come from? Does it come from the straight line? Does the straight line essentially create certainty and shows you how to transfer it to your prospect? It doesn't make sense. Think about that. The straight line system itself allows you to transfer certainty to the prospect. You transfer certainty. But where does the certainty come from? The certainty comes from within you. In other words, at the beginning of a sale, you, the sales person, you are absolutely certain. You're like this white hot certainty. You're damn sure your product is unbelievable. Best things in sliced berry. You love what you got. And it oozes out of you. Now, again, we have different things like tonality and body language. It allows us to express that. But what I'm trying to say here is that it starts with you, that you are in a state of certainty. And what's that? That's part of the inner game of success to manage your emotional state. That's why if you're in a state of uncertainty, no matter how good you are at applying the straight line, you really can't close at a high level. You get that? And there's, you have to be in a state of certainty. You're absolutely certain about your product. And then what you do is you transfer that certainty to the prospect using the straight line. So the perfect analogy here would be that essentially imagine you have a home, right? So in the home, you have a furnace, right? And the furnace creates heat. But how does the heat get to the rest of the rooms in the house? Well, you have an HVAC, some ductwork, right? And the ductwork is what allows the heat to flow from a furnace through the various parts of the house that don't need to be heated. So it elegantly flows to the individual places. It directs the flow of the energy of the heat and warms up the room. So in this case, in the metaphor, the straight line system is like the ductwork. It's the HVAC system. And you are the furnace. So if the furnace is not lit up, if the furnace is cold, what happens? I don't care how great the piping might be the most efficient piping in the world, right? Perfect. No leaks. It's just right. Nothing to transfer. So what happens is in the real world, we can't just focus on success, meaning to master the tactics and techniques of the straight line. We also have to master the inner game of success, your ability to manage your state, the beliefs that you have about success. Because again, if you don't have the proper beliefs and power beliefs, you don't act. If you say, I'm not good at sales or selling is evil, those are beliefs, they'll stop you from taking action, right? Also if your standards are too low, you have low standards, what happens? Well, if your standards are low and you learn the straight line, guess what happens? You will end up making money really easily, but you'll stop working and say, oh, I worked a day, wow, look at that. I closed the window, I'll stop. You'll not keep working because we always rise to a fall to our standard. That standard is your level of, it's your must, that thermostat, where you burn, right? So the point is, this first element is to remember, always remember when you look at your own success and you're saying, you know, am I doing as well as I can? That's number one, okay? And with that, that's one to number two, okay? And this is a great one now. Let's get into the nitty gritty here, right? One of the things I say about the straight line, I have this great example I use, they talk about the two types of selling. And this is one of the great things about the idiocy of a lot of the sales training programs that are out there. They focus on like two things. They say, you know, there's the old sales model, which is like that hard charging, rah, Graham by the throat and close them model, right? Called the old model. And then they have the new way of selling, which is a consultative soft approach. Let me explain to you what those two models are and we'll see what's what, right? So in the old model of selling, okay, you have that's the hard charging sort of like the perceived like the Glenn Gary, Glenn Gary, Glenn Russell, Wolf of Walsh, you'd be like, just, rah, just rip their throats out, right? In that model, what they say is you want to spend basically 10% of your time building rapport, right? 20% of your time and I'm not just, you know, how should he have a distance of the artist, right? 20% of your time identifying a qualifying, right? 30% of your time, okay, presenting and this should be 40% right? And 40% of your time closing. In other words, the bulk of what you're doing in the old model is you're just the hard charging closing machine who pays very little attention to getting, you know, you're getting to rapport and that's it, you're done, right? And this is what it's all about. Close, close, close, close, close, ABC always be closed. That's the old model of selling, right? And then we on the other side of the equation have what's called the new model or the consultative model. And what they do here is they kind of flip things that upside on its head. They say it's 40% of what you do is about building relationships. 40% is building relationships, which is basically another name for getting into being a rapport people. They say you want to spend 30% of the time identifying needs, which is qualifying. They want to say you have 20% of your time offering solutions and 10% of your time closing, confirming and closing. That's the consultative model. And I have to tell you that this is so idiotically ridiculous, so ridiculous. I mean, if you spend 10% of your time closing, you'll be broke and you'll be out of the game. You can't do that because the fact is you're always closing. But here's the other part that's ironic is that this model is just as stupid. Here's the truth and here's what the straight line is about. With the straight line system, you're always doing all things at once. You're not getting into rapport 10%, 20%. You always have to be building rapport. Remember, rapport is not a constant. If at any point during a sale, you slip out of rapport, you're screwed. You can't influence. Right? So in other words, rapport, they say one says 10%, the other one says 40%. I'll take it to 100%. You're always building rapport, maintaining rapport, and you have almost an eagle eye saying if you feel yourself sliding out of rapport, you need to jump in and fix that shit right away and get yourself back in rapport because you can't move forward on the straight line without being in rapport. It's a must. Right? Second aspect, asking questions. It's a gathering intelligence, identifying needs. Whatever you want to call it, either model. Right? You never stop asking questions. In other words, sales is about asking questions and identifying solutions based on those questions you ask. But the point is, it's not like I'm going to ask questions for this little part and I'm never going to know. Remember, looping, what are we doing? We're constantly asking people questions. Does it make sense to you? We never stop. We're always asking questions. So again, when it comes to identifying needs or asking questions, we're doing that 100% of the time as well. Never stops. Right? Presenting our case or presenting our solution. Right? Guess what? Okay? I think it's a 30 and then it's 20 with the base which one you have. Right? Here's the deal. Ready? With the straight line? 100% of the time you're offering solutions. From the first word out of your mouth, that's your solution. You know why? You're an expert. And experts are the one that give people solutions. It's not like I can just sort of like kind of be like kind of rambling and not be presenting and kind of have this kind of free time where I kind of free form and then only in this defined moment in the preso, this is where my actual script is. In fact, that's the reason why when you look at untrained salespeople or sales forces, if you say let me see your script, they'll hand you something that's only for like the actual logical case of their presentation and they think that's their script. And that's why they can't fucking close because what happens is that's not a present. Your presentation starts when from the first word out of your mouth, you're presenting and every word, there are no free words, no student statements, right? No time to go off the Pluto or the top of the presidency in China. Every word you say is directed. That's power. It's got meaning. Right? And that meaning is to what? Create massive certainty on every level for the three tens, right? And then lastly, this idea about closing. They say it's 40% of the time with the old model and 10% of the time. No more. Guess what? I'm closing from the first word out of my mouth and you heard me say this many times before. I'm not closing you on buying, but I'm closing you on that I'm an expert and worth listening to. I'm closing you that you should open up to me and answer my questions honestly and forthrightedly. I'm closing you on taking the time to listen to my solution and I'm closing you ultimately on buying from me. I never stop closing. ABC. Always be closing. The mistake that people make with this thing, it means that you're just, you're a hard charging animal about the end. It's not that. It means that you're always closing the prospect on something. You know, in marketing, we say that, you know, essentially in marketing is about getting someone to take the next step. Well, the same thing goes with sales. Everything you say is designed when I say, you know, you move someone down the straight line from the open to the close, it's step, step, everything is slowly pushing them down the straight line and that's what happens. So is it the old model? No, they're both bullshit. They really are. There's only one large cohesive model, which is a straight line because you're doing all things at the same time. Now, okay, so these, they start to get a repetitive fear. So let me skip here to another, because I start covering them. I start naturally jumping at it. Right. He has a big one. All right. You're going to love this. This is a very important one. At the heart of every decision that we make as human beings is something called the law of positive intent. In other words, human beings, we do things. We decide because we think our decision will enhance our lives in someone. We don't do things that we think will make our life worse. You follow me? We say, yes, we think it will make our life better. And we'll say, no, we think it'll make it worse. Or we will say, yes, we think it will cause us pleasure. And we say, no, we think it will cause us pain. But what does that mean with the straight line? Well, think about this. We might talk about you have a certain scale from one to a 10. And a one is uncertainty, and this is absolute certainty. But I say to you, this is really important about deciding who you should take down the straight line. What I say to you is this. When you made a presentation, let's say you've got out of qualifying, and you've made your presentation. If you say, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? If someone comes in at a three or two or 3.5 and they're saying, yeah, not really. I don't really like. Does the idea make sense? Do you like the idea? They're saying, no, not really. And then you run a loop on them. You run a loop on them, and they're still like a four. If they don't like your idea, they will never buy from you. In other words, below a five means that it's no longer ambivalence. It's now like, no, I don't really like what you have. So we do not want to ever move forward on the straight line with people that fall below a five. So those of you asked that first magic question, let me get those ideas. It makes sense to do you like it and say, no, not really. I don't really see what you see, Jordan. I know I have a problem here. So in other words, what I'm not going to do is I will run a loop. And so we just simply just cannot interpret what you're saying. All right. So it's a crucial aspect that human beings only make decisions based on positive intent. Now that doesn't mean they're always right. Very often people make decisions and they end up being stupid decisions, but they thought those decisions would benefit them. That's the difference. Okay. So just make sure, remember that when you're essentially selling someone, when you're persuading someone, what you're looking to do is you, this is five represents ambivalence. Now, something four or five months ago, I was walking on the beach and I was doing a training on looping and I came up with this, like kind of this statement, certainty ways. And I've said that many times before, certainty, ways, but I started using that as a way of explaining why we loop. And in fact, I call it certainty range, wains, which is why we like hold that. That's right. How certainty ways, which is why we loop. So let me go through this because this is again, is another overarching equations. Just it's human nature. And because certainty, wains, I'll explain what that means in a second. That's why we loop. What I mean by certainty wains is that you give awesome presentation to someone, right? I mean, just get it out of the park, right? And you sum up all the benefits and your future pace. And when you're done, you did such a good job and you say, well, let me ask you, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? They say, oh my, yes, joy. I'm excited. Yeah, it's great. It's a perfect group. I love the idea. They're like at a 9.5 or 10, right? That's their absolute, you've gotten to a state of absolute certainty, right? Four minutes later, they're back at a 8.5. The certainty drops. It wanes. It doesn't last ever. The only thing that lasts forever is a conviction. And that's a very different thing. A conviction is a tacit belief. Like you're so fucking sure that it's unshakable, never drops, but it takes a long time for someone to get to a point that's a conviction. And it's very, very hard to do and it's in interest sales, so to speak, right? Example, right now I have a conviction that I'm a great salesperson. No one's going to shake that belief. I know I am. And by, excuse me, it's right or wrong. In my heart and my mind, I'm sure, right? Here's another conviction, more appropriate. I think that iPhones are the best. No one's going to convince me. I mean, I'm not going to be convinced at this point to switch to Samsung. And by the way, they're great too. I know that Samsung's a good too, so I'm a good friend. But for me, I love my iPhones. I'm sure of it. That's what I buy. That's what I use. Same thing goes with my car, driver Mercedes. I'm convinced. I'm sure of that. It took me many years to get there, but now I'm sure. That's a 10 on the certainty scale. Most of the time, with most of the products that you sell, and I don't know what you're exactly selling, you can't get someone to an absolute 10. That's a conviction, right? They're just short. You get them to a 9.6, where they're almost there. But as soon as you stop talking and those seeds of doubt start creeping back in, they start to fall. Certainty wanes. Which is why when I loop, so watch what happens. Let's say I go, let's move this here, watch. Let's say I move someone down the straight line. The reason I'm doing that now is I can no longer lift my hand up high enough. Believe it or not, to flip over the page. I can't do it anymore. It's fucking stuck. My arm, right? It's out of the socket, right? That's why I'm doing that. So here, watch. You have it open and you have it closed, right? Here's your dotted lines, as far as I can go. So watch. Here's your first four seconds. Here's what you do with your intelligence gathering report building. You do all things at all times, of course. We make our presentation. We ask for the order for the first time. And let's say when you do it, they say they want to think about. They say, well, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And they say, that sounds pretty good, right? So that's the basis for your first loop. You're going to loop back, okay? And you're going to resell the idea. And then you'll say, again, does that make you see it now? They say, oh, yeah, it's great. It's awesome. So that's your first part of your loop, right? So you're like, check. You've gotten, let's say, a nine on the certainty scale. Then you say, well, let me ask you this. If I've been your broker, I've been your advisor. You've been working there for the last few years, and I made you money in a consistent basis, then you probably wouldn't be saying, let me think about it right now. This is called your what? Your forest gump bag, right? And you're going to resell yourself, right? And as you're doing that, you're reselling yourself. Guess what's happening? As honest as it seems, you're building up certainty for yourself, and this is already going, it's already starting to do a dive. Not a nose dive, it's a subtle drop off to a nine. To an 8.7. Certainty wanes. Someone says, oh yeah, I love the idea. And then, like a microsecond later, those seeds are stuck, wait a second, do I really like, they doubt themselves, how we are, we doubt ourselves, right? So what happens is, is as you're running a loop, you're almost like finding two forces at once. You're finding the battle of whatever can you're focused on in that moment, and you're also aware that the previous 10 is starting to slip. So what that means is, at the very end, before we close, we'll do one last button up of each of the three 10s. We button things up one last time, and then we ask for the order. This is a crucial aspect to succeeding with the straight line. In other words, we remember that the cause certainty wanes, we're always, it's like, we're fighting this, decaying force of certainty that naturally occurs. You didn't do anything wrong, it's just how human beings are built. So that's, so why that's an overarching equation is because that force, that natural force of nature, is why the straight line operates the way it operates. That's why we loop back, and we keep reinforcing, reselling the first 10, the second 10, the third 10. We don't just say, check, I got the first 10 resolved, I don't have to talk about that again. No, when we run our next loop, we're gonna go back and resell the first 10 again, not as in depth, but we need to button back up because what happens is, let's say we got into a 9.5, and now, naturally, we're running our second, and if they drop back down to an 8.5, I wanna make sure I hit this again and bring them back up to a 9.7 even, a little higher, before I move forward on the line. You have to go back and reinforce what you did. And when you start doing that, when you start really understanding that you're always sort of fighting this decay, this natural decay of certainty, it gives you the sort of the context to realize why you run the patterns the way you run them, why you always sum things up one last time at the end, it's because you're buttoning up certainty for everything, for all three 10s, all right? It's an important overarching equation, and it governs whether or not you get a close or not, all right? Let's move forward to the next one here. Take a good one here, got a bunch. Here's the biggest one of all, right? Okay. This is the equation, and I've mentioned this to you a couple of times, and I don't think I've really ever dug into it as deeply as I'd like, and this is what's called your energy in benefits out equation, all right? And to do this, I'm gonna tell you a really funny story, let me tell you a story that will drive this home. Many years ago, I had a problem with my tooth, right? I had this, even in college, I had an infection, they drilled, anyway, I tried over the years to save this tooth and did for like 10, 12 years, and I kept getting a root canal, and then another root canal, and then this and that, and eventually, it got to the point where the tooth had to go, right? It just had to go, right? So I'll go to the dentist, right, and he yanks out this tooth, right, and I'm gonna now have space now, and now I have to go to make a decision of what kind of implant I want. Do I want a regular full blown implant, or do I want like what's called a bridge, two options? And the dentist starts to explain to me about, okay, here's what it is to give an implant, and here's how you go about getting a bridge, and he explains that with the bridge, okay, you come here and I'll create some sort of like, you know, artificial thing, and it hooks to the back tooth, to the front tooth, and I put it on, right, and it simples that, and you're done, right? It goes, the only problem with that is a bridge doesn't really last forever. It's kind of like, you know, it could crack when you're eating one day, and it's not like sort of like, you'll always be a little bit conscious that you have a bridge. The other alternative is you can go get a full blown dental implant, because now if you get an implant, okay, here's what has to happen, you're gonna come in, now it takes you three or four trips to the dentist, I'm like, oh my fucking, I'm like three or four trips to the dentist, right? He goes, and in the first trip I'm gonna do is I have to prepare your bone there, because your bone's a bit weak. So I'm gonna put some pigs fucking bone, like what, like pigs bone, who I couldn't believe it, you put some stuff there to help make your bone tighter and stronger, right? It goes, then once it's there, you'll come back, and we're gonna have, send you to the oral surgeon who's gonna screw an implant in there, like put a screw in there, then we have to wait three to four months till that screw sets, right? Once that screw is fully set in there, okay, then we'll come back and we can finally fit you for this tooth here, and put this tooth, screw this tooth back in, and once it's there, it's permanent. I'm like, what the fuck? I'm like four trips to the dentist, and I can't even believe this. Now, just so you know, I ended up going and getting an implant, but not from this dentist. I said, let me think about it. That's, I said, and when I went home, did I think, I didn't think about shit, I'm like, this is crazy. What the guy did, okay, is he essentially created a scenario for me where I had to expend all this type of energy and to go back to the dentist, to the oral surgeon, under anesthesia, back to the dentist, back to the, a one-year process because an implant will last forever. And I just, I couldn't, in my own mind, I had to expend too much energy, and then when he told me the price, he goes, oh, an implant will cost you four grand, a bridge is only $800. So I'm thinking, I should take the bridge. I mean, like, but here's the deal. The truth is, is that the implant is much better for you. Implant is a tooth you'll have for a lifetime, much better you want the implant, right? But the way this guy positioned it, the way he explained it, I'm looking at this as massive energy expenditure on the way in. I gotta keep going back to the dentist, getting in the car, driving there, wasting my time. And then of course, money is the ultimate form of stored energy, right? You work, you spend your money to live, to, you know, food, shelter, clothing, right? The excess you have you put in the bank, that's stored energy from your hard work. And then you can essentially deploy that energy, deploy the stored energy, how you see fit. So when you do that, when you deploy that energy, right? Well, you know, that energy had a cost of your hard work to it, right? So I wanna make sure that when I deploy energy, I direct energy that the benefits on the other side are much greater. So imagine if you set up a closing scenario, so the guy explains to me about the whole teeth, the whole thing, my options, and he sends up the scenario, and to me, it sounds like I have to put in all this fricking energy, spend a lot of money to get the implant, and he didn't really explain to me why the implant was better, what's the benefits out? Now, if this guy knew even this much about the straight line system, what he would have done is said, listen, it's very simple, it's a few trips to the doctor, it's a bit of a pain, yes, no big deal, okay? We make you comfortable, you come in, we'll put the screw in here, you're under no pain, there's no pain felt at all, okay? And we'll give you a temporary tooth in there so it doesn't look bad while you're waiting, and once it's all done, you'll know you have a tooth for life. You'll be able to smile freely, eat any type of food you want, and when you're 65, 70 years old, trust me, you'll be glad you had this. It's only $5,000, Jordan, trust me, it might seem like a bit of money here, but believe me, when you analyze that over the long term, over throughout your life, it's the only way to go. Does that sound good? I would have said, no, I don't expect a dentist to do it as good as that, but I would say, yeah, I mean, fucking yeah, right? But because this dentist didn't explain it, he didn't essentially, when he closed, tried to close me, so to speak, right? He did not sum it up at the end, at the very end, we always make this decision, like basically, is it worth it? At the end of the day, is it worth it? If I have to expend a certain amount of energy, meaning go to the dentist, go home, go back to the dentist, go home, three, four trips to the dentist, right? $5,000, being uncomfortable, all this energy, and then what do I get? A permanent tooth? It sounds like the bridge is almost as good. Didn't know how to explain it. Now, this comes into play in every single sale, I don't care what you sell. The moment before, or the moment after you ask for the order, what you have is you have this moment where the prospect just instantly sums up all the benefits, and it's more, what are the benefits here, and how much is it gonna cost? So the last thing he heard was, it's $7,000, and you just say, he goes, oh, it's at $7,000? That's the last word, the last thing he keys in on is $7,000 of energy. So you would never close like that. Or you would never close by saying, oh, getting started, sir, you've come down, you have to sign the application, go to the post office, put a self-adjust, put a stamp on it, then go back, then go get the notary, make sure the document is notarized, then go home, jump through a hoop of fire, I'm exaggerating, you get the point here. You would never take someone, make them go through seven to 10 steps, and then say, and it's $7,000, they'll never get a buy like that. So what you say is getting started is very simple, it's a question of some basic information, we do most of the work for you, and again, you're gonna get benefit ABC, benefit DEF, benefit, benefit, benefit, benefit, and believe me, you will be glad you did. Sound fair enough? So you always wanna lay out your closing scenarios by essentially minimizing the amount of energy expended on the way in, getting started is simple, question of basic information, right? And then on the way out, say and you're gonna get massive benefit, benefit, benefit, and believe me, you'll be glad you did. If you set it up that way, you have an excellent shot of closing. Now the same thing goes when it comes to buy signals. All right now, Chancey, move the fuck out of my, it's a perfect time, move away. You're sitting right behind the camera, I'm seeing your face, okay? Now the same thing goes when it comes to, when you get buy signals in the back half of the sale. What do I mean? Now there's a buy signal is when you ask for the order and rather than getting an objection or yes, you get what's called a buy signal. Say, how much does that cost again? That's a buy signal. Okay, how long will the delivery take? That's a buy signal, right? So someone says, what's the cost again? You say, oh, it's $5,000. Eh, you just lost. See that's an example where you say, oh, it's $5,000. No, see, oh, it's only $5,000. And again, what you're gonna get is benefit, benefit, benefit, and benefit, benefit, benefit, and you sum up the benefits and again, getting starts very simple and believe me, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Does that sound fair enough? You get it, you don't, I can't leave you an open loop. Say, how much is it? Oh, it's $6,000. It's like, eh, the movie prize, you're done. Because what you did is you said it's $6,000 for your hard earned money. It's all the energy of sort of like, they're like, fuck. What does it mean really? Well, another way is that people have really short memories when it comes to the benefits. You could have told them like certainty wanes, benefits wane, you know, you sum up all the benefits three minutes later, like what benefits? They forgot. In the face of money, expending energy, the form of money, it trumps things unless you restate the benefits. The company, and let me say this, most companies really get this role. But the one company that really gets this right better than anybody out there, and because of that, it's allowed them to grow and become like the largest company in the world, amazon.com. Think about it, one click purchasing. Don't have to enter your credit card again. Don't have to even enter it, click, and dive into a second page, no. Because what they found out is that if you have to go to even one more page, one more click, they lose 40% of the people. And if an even another click, another 30% on top of that. That's how profound it is, even one freaking click of a mouse, because every time you click, it's energy. So what they do is one click buying, you're a prime member, we already have your credit card on file, it's so simple to do. It feels sometimes like you just bought something without spending any money. You live it right to your door, so simple. That's what's allowed amazon to become amazon. They make things easy, it's brilliant, really it's brilliant technology, and they do it so well. So always remember, any time you're setting up a closing scenario, remember to adhere to this equation. Energy in, benefits out. Essentially if the energy that you have to expend is above the benefits, here's your benefits, right? Here, they won't buy. You could have done everything right up to that point, they won't buy. But when the benefits start to go above the energy they expend, it's positive energy. You're in the positive, essentially you're in the green, so to speak, bam, you set this up to close. Now remember, just because you're positive in that equation, doesn't mean they'll buy. There's other reasons why they still might not buy. But everything else could have been right, and because you forgot this one little thing, you're screwed, they don't buy. So it's sort of that over-arture equation at the end of the day, before you ask for the order, always say to yourself, have I done my energy in benefits out, have I framed this in a way that they naturally will understand that the benefits I get exceed the energy, or in layman's terms, you know what? At the end of the day, it's just fricking worth it. It's worth it, makes sense, that's what it's about, okay? Time for one more here, how long we going, that's the phone? What do you think, how long we going for, so far? For a minute. Okay, let's go, we got something, we got two more here, okay? Here's another great one here, okay? It has to do with about, it's an over-arture equation about never letting your guard down. Here's the deal. We judge books by their covers, right? We make snap decisions, we see someone, we take them in, we rip each piece of them apart, and then we put them back together based on how they're perceived. If we perceive someone as being sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in the field, right? It opens us up to say, you know what? This person could probably help us achieve our goals, help us get what we want. So we listen, we defer, we essentially give control to those who are experts in their field, right? That's kind of an obvious thing. It's the basis of the straight line. It's why we must capture their attention in those first four seconds in exactly the right way. And if you do that, it allows us to take control of the conversation and move them slowly, but surely, step-by-step down the straight line, right? Basic, right? But here's the point where people fall down. They perfect the art of sounding really good in those first four seconds. And even the 10 seconds after that, right? But imagine, remember, you have a long sale here. You know, how long does the sale take? Well, I can't really tell you because all of your businesses are different, but maybe it takes somewhere between eight and 30 minutes, right, to make a sale. Here's the deal. This is almost the full, watch your questions, the whole thing. If at any point along this continuum, you are revealed as a novice, you're screwed. You can never let your guard down. There is no amount of expert currency where you could build, like you build up so much currency that you're an expert that now you can say stupid shit. It never works. One reveal, the human mind is lightning fast. Even one reveal that, wait a second, things are not as they seem. There's something about this person. There's an incongruency. It's enough to poison the sale. So when I talk about these first four seconds, right, that you must capture someone's attention, position yourself sharp as it's at, enthusiastically have an expert in your field, right? I don't just mean then that you can let your guard down and be average, be okay. You can never be perceived as anything less than a total expert in your field. Remember how it works. In the first four seconds, it's a gut reaction, right? You know, someone perceives you the right way. It's a gut reaction they have, right? Fast and they can think it's not the conscious mind. It's an unconscious mind based on a pattern. So you hit a pattern that says, you know what? This is how experts sound. This is what experts say. This is how experts look. And because that you're perceived the right way, right? That's an unconscious reaction, lightning fast. But then what happens is the conscious mind now takes over and it starts saying, hmm, is this congruent? In other words, it starts to almost like it's the, the conscious mind is the inspector. It's looking for some, is there, is my being lied to? Did someone try to pull the wool over my eyes? The conscious mind is very suspicious. The unconscious mind is not suspicious. It just bam, it makes a decision. You have all this information stored away since you were a big, your conscious mind remembers everything and based on the patterns that you've formed and laid down, it looks for similarities. It says when someone is dressed this way, sounds this way and acts this way, they fall into what? The expert bucket. That's, and it puts you in the expert bucket. No thought required. But as a self-protective mechanism now, as the sale progresses, their conscious mind, they're looking, they're looking for that one inconsistent, that one incongruency to say, ah, it wasn't real. Knowing that, it means that you have to basically have the entire sale mapped out from front to back and that gets to the main and final point, which is it's at the end of the day where it really drives a straight line is strategic preparation, strategic prep. Meaning that before you enter any sales encounter, you need to map out in your mind and on paper, right? All the possible eventualities, all the scenarios and have answers and patterns and scripts that allow you to go from open to close all the way through and giving yourself as many options as possible. In other words, it's not enough just to have one aspect of the screen. You need to have the whole thing locked down from front to back because at any point in time, remember, and here's another archer, I sort of mentioned this for another overarching law, is that rapport is not a constant. Right? You could be in total rapport and once you break it, it's gone, it's not like you're in rapport and now I'm good now, no, there's no constant. You can be an ultra tight rapport and you say something and it breaks it and you can't close. So in other words, it's running the entire length of the, when I say it's an overarching, it means it runs the entire length of the straight line. You always need to be in rapport, which means, here's the thing, you're not gonna always be in rapport, which means if you fall out, you have to get back in. So in other words, you can't move forward on the line when you're out of rapport, you just can't. So this whole idea that you're perceived the right way and that opens you up to taking control and when you take control and you ask questions and you use your active listening to get into that ultra tight rapport with the, huh, mm-hmm, well that right, you get into that ultra tight rapport, but once you're there, it's not like, okay, check rapport is done, I can be an asshole now. At any point in time, based on the last words that you said and whether the prospect agreed with you or disagreed with you, whether they resonated with it or didn't resonate with it, right? That rapport will kind of go up and down, it varies. And if you say something stupid or something offensive or something that reveals you from being a novice, the rapport falls off a cliff and you can't close. And you're revealed as a novice and for all the other reasons, you get it, it's basically a death spiral, all right? So when I talk about the straight line, why it works so well, at the heart of it are these overarching, essentially the human condition basically. These are just how people are built, how we operate, how we perceive reality. So the straight line essentially allows you to go from the open to the close without violating these fundamental rules that human beings go by when they make decisions. So I want you to go through this, it's strange when you can really review again and again. And also, if any particular one of these things is something that is problematic, I promise you I've covered it in another training. Go back to that training where it was one hour just on that topic and dive into it. And by doing that, you simply set yourself up to be able to close anyone's closeable by not violating the rules that knock you out of the game. You can do everything right and you violate one of these overarching rules and you're screwed. But here's the deal, right? What you found here is that when you started with this, you closed it with X, it's gone up a lot, I'm sure it has, right? Dramatically so. The reason is, is because in the beginning you were violating a lot of these rules. Now you've knocked out most of them. You get it, you've made massive progress and most of you made an incredible progress. But here's the deal, that highest level to be able to close anyone who's closeable, that happens when you run a full straight line pattern without violating any of the rules. You do that, you're at that point where you truly can close anyone who's closeable and again, it doesn't mean you close everyone but you walk away from the sale saying, you know what, they didn't buy, but nobody. I mean, no one could have closed them and that's an amazing feeling. You have that feeling, you're gonna wanna just seize the day, you're gonna wanna just go out and talk to anyone because you'll know that if there's any shot, you'll close. That's the bottom line, right?
